title: Wazuh Alert Investigation
summary: Student investigates an instructor-provided Wazuh alert or sample alert and documents triage, evidence, and disposition.
student_goal: Review a security alert, identify the affected asset and event details, and document whether it is benign, suspicious, or requires escalation.

required_evidence.label: Alert source
required_evidence.description: Student must state whether the alert came from the live Wazuh dashboard, an instructor-provided screenshot, or a sample alert.

required_evidence.label: Alert details
required_evidence.description: Student must provide alert title, rule ID, severity, timestamp, and source system.

required_evidence.label: Affected asset
required_evidence.description: Student must identify the affected endpoint, user, service, or monitored agent.

required_evidence.label: Disposition
required_evidence.description: Student must classify the alert as benign, suspicious, or requiring escalation with reasoning.

student_workflow: Open the Zammad ticket and identify the alert source provided by the instructor.
Open ARIA AI Mentor and load the assigned ticket.
Review the Wazuh alert or sample alert without exposing secrets, tokens, or private credentials.
Collect rule ID, severity, timestamp, affected asset, and observable event details.
Document whether the alert is benign, suspicious, or requires escalation with evidence-based reasoning.
opening_questions: Was this alert from the live Wazuh dashboard or an instructor-provided sample?
What rule ID, severity, timestamp, and source system are shown?
Which host, user, service, or agent is affected?
What evidence supports benign, suspicious, or escalation status?
mentor_guidance_rules: Do not close a security alert without evidence and disposition.
Ask for alert source, rule ID, severity, asset, and timestamp before conclusion.
Escalate if evidence suggests compromise or if impact is unclear.
Do not ask students to paste secrets, tokens, passwords, or private credentials.
